summary: DHH uses the persistence of cookie banners as evidence that the EU is fundamentally incapable of effective tech regulation. He argues that billions spent on cookie banner compliance have produced no meaningful privacy improvements, yet the EU has no mechanism to self-correct this legislative failure. He extends this critique to newer regulations like the Digital Markets Act and AI legislation, suggesting that if the EU can't get something as basic as cookies right, more complex regulation is doomed. He concludes that Europe's regulatory approach is actively squandering its entrepreneurial potential and driving innovation elsewhere.
key_points: Cookie banners cost billions in compliance while providing no material privacy improvement to users
The EU lacks any mechanism for self-correcting legislative failures, even with years of evidence
Europe's AI legislation is already drawing second thoughts from its own architect, following the same pattern of vague, overreaching regulation
Europe's tech innovation is largely incubation-stage, with companies leaving for America once they're ready to scale
The cookie banner failure should serve as a cautionary lens for evaluating all EU tech legislation
money_quotes: Companies have spent billions on cookie banner compliance only to endlessly annoy users with no material improvement to their privacy.
The bureaucratic maze almost guarantees that all the noble intentions eventually find a dead end in which to get stuck.
If the EU can screw up something as basic as cookie banners this bad, and prove so incapable and uninterested in re-calibrating its approach, what hope is there for the thornier issues?
Like many modern European laws, it's a vague, posturing, and suffocating mess that's way too specific about things it fails to prove the ability to understand.
So little of the core tech innovation that's driving the future, in AI or otherwise, is happening in Europe.
If you want different outcomes, you have to start doing different things.
key_insight: The EU's inability to fix its own cookie banner failure after years of evidence reveals a regulatory system structurally incapable of governing technology effectively.
